name: One-Pan Chicken Thighs with Burst Tomatoes, Harissa & Feta
why: Quick weeknight dinner with bold North African harissa flavor — peak summer cherry tomatoes burst into a smoky, tangy sauce in one pan. No chopping required.
author: Henry
ingredients: 6 skin-on, bone-in chicken thighs (about 2¼ lb. total)

steps: Pat chicken thighs dry with paper towels — this is key for crispy skin.
Season all over with 2½ tsp. salt (about 1 tsp. per pound of meat).
Place chicken skin-side down in a dry large cast-iron skillet with the heat off.
Turn heat to medium and cook undisturbed until skin is very crisp, deeply golden brown, and releases from the pan, 13–16 minutes.
Rotate the pan (not the chicken) occasionally to ensure even browning across all thighs.
Using tongs, transfer chicken to a plate skin-side up — never let the crispy skin touch the plate or it will steam and go soggy.
Add cherry tomatoes, harissa paste, red wine vinegar, 1 oregano sprig, and remaining ½ tsp. salt to the same pan.
Increase heat to medium-high and cook, stirring occasionally, until tomatoes have burst and released all their juices, and the juices have started to thicken, 8–10 minutes.
Nestle chicken thighs back into the skillet skin-side up over the tomatoes.

Reduce heat to medium-low and simmer until chicken is just cooked through (instant-read thermometer at 165°F near the bone) and sauce has thickened enough that a wooden spoon leaves a trail when dragged through, 6–8 minutes.
Let chicken rest in the pan for 5 minutes — this lets the meat reabsorb surface moisture for tender, juicy results.
While chicken rests, cut feta into ¼"-thick planks.
Break feta planks into large pieces and scatter over the skillet — some will stay intact, others will melt into the sauce.
Pick leaves off remaining 2 oregano sprigs and scatter over everything.
Serve immediately with torn crusty bread for sopping up the tomato sauce.

pro_tips: Starting chicken skin-side down in a room-temp pan (not preheated) guarantees the crispiest skin — the fat renders slowly and evenly instead of burning.
Taste your harissa before adding it — brands vary wildly in heat. NY SHUK is a solid mid-heat choice; adjust quantity to your spice preference.
